global_rules: Never include aspect ratio in the prompt — set it directly in the tool's UI
Never use the word 'slider' for camera movement
Describe what you want to appear, not what to avoid — use positive framing (e.g. 'empty street' not 'no cars')
Never use command words like 'generate', 'create', or 'make' — they are not visual cues
Be hyper-specific about materials and surfaces: never say 'jacket', say 'navy blue tweed jacket'; never say 'mug', say 'minimalist ceramic coffee mug'
Prompts should be descriptive, not conversational — do not include pleasantries, questions, or requests like 'can you please generate'. Just describe the visual
Use positive phrasing only — never describe what should NOT appear (e.g. 'a bald man' not 'a man with no hair'). Negative phrasing may produce the opposite result

nano_banana.output_format: Output a single prompt string. Use the Gen-4 Image formula: [Subject + strong action verb] + [Location/Context] + [Composition/Framing] + [Lighting] + [Style/Aesthetic]. Use full sentences for precise control, and append style keywords where additional variation is desired. Be hyper-specific about materials, lens, lighting, and composition. The prompt will be pasted directly into RunwayML's Gen-4 Image interface.
must_include: Strong subject + action verb opening (not a vague noun like 'A bottle' — instead 'A bottle catches golden light')
Explicit lighting description naming the technique (not just 'lit' or 'bright')
At least one named material or surface texture
Composition / framing language (rule of thirds, negative space, leading lines, etc.)
A defined visual style or aesthetic
must_avoid: Stacking more than 2 distinct style references — quality degrades quickly above the 1–2 style limit
Vague nouns without action verbs — every prompt should open with subject + verb
Generic lighting words ('nice lighting', 'good light') — name the technique

aesthetic_styles: dreamy (soft focus, light leaks, ethereal glow)

examples.instruction: A vintage typewriter on a desk by a sunlit window, moody
examples.good_output: A weathered black Underwood typewriter rests centered on a worn oak desk, with a single half-typed page curling out of the carriage. Behind it, a tall sash window pours golden hour light across the desktop, casting long warm shadows over scattered loose-leaf paper, a brass desk lamp, and a chipped ceramic mug. Shot on an 85mm portrait lens with shallow depth of field, the background dissolving into soft bokeh of dust motes drifting through the air. Side-lit chiaroscuro lighting carves the typewriter keys in sharp relief. Kodak film color grading, warm tones, melancholy mood, editorial photography aesthetic.
examples.why: Strong subject + verb opening ('typewriter rests', 'light pours'), named materials (weathered oak, brass, chipped ceramic), specified lens (85mm portrait), named lighting (side-lit chiaroscuro, golden hour), film stock (Kodak), composition (centered, shallow DOF), no negative phrasing, no artist names, no command verbs.

rules: Gen-4 Image prompts support a wide variety of structures, keywords, and styles. Promptable elements: Subject, Scene, Composition, Lighting, Color, Style, Focus, Angle, Text, Mood
Detailed prompts give fine-tuned control — use full sentences for precise control over elements, and append additional keywords where more variation is welcomed
Lighting is the single most impactful element — always specify it
For transparency and reflective surfaces, call them out explicitly: 'glass bottle with visible caustic light refraction', 'chrome surface with environment reflection'
Specify product placement with compositional language: 'placed in the lower-right corner using rule of thirds', 'centered in negative space', 'foreground left with blurred background'

For text in images: enclose desired text in quotes and describe the rendering style — e.g. 'neon sign that spells "RUNWAY"', 'theater marquee that boldly spells "FRAMES"', 'balloons forming the word "HELLO"'
Reference specific color grading and film stock when relevant
For subject poses: 'relaxed pose', 'natural motion blur emphasizes movement', 'curves and bends impossibly' (surreal anatomy)

veo.skeleton: [Camera shot] of [subject/object] [active verb action] in [environment]. [Lighting]. [Audio if enabled]. [Style upfront].
veo.output_format: Output a single prompt block of dense cinematographic detail. Use the Runway structure: [Camera] shot of [subject/object] [action] in [environment]. [Supporting descriptions]. Describe both visual elements AND motion. Use precise camera terminology (push in, dolly, tracking, orbit, etc.). The prompt will be pasted directly into RunwayML's video generation interface.
must_include: Exactly ONE primary camera motion (multiple competing motions cause the model to improvise)
Active subject-verb action — never passive constructions
Environment + atmosphere using sensory and atmospheric language
Lighting: source, direction, quality, color temperature
Visual style defined upfront (cinematic / animation / 35mm film noir / handheld documentary / etc.)
Audio design — ambient soundscape, dialogue, mechanical sounds, music style (when audio is enabled)
Add 'no subtitles' at the end if you do not want text overlaid on the video
must_avoid: Multiple competing camera motions in one prompt — pick ONE primary motion
Passive verbs ('there was a man standing') — always use active subject-verb
Vague action ('doing things', 'interacting', 'moving around') — give exact play-by-play
Ambiguous dialogue — either specify exact lines or say 'character speaks about [topic]'

examples.instruction: A barista pulling an espresso shot, slow-mo dramatic feel
examples.good_output: Tracking macro shot of a barista's hand tilting a chrome portafilter into a stainless steel espresso machine, slow-motion at 240fps. Dark crema crowns the shot glass as ribbons of espresso spiral down through the polished spout. Warm pendant lighting reflects off the brushed metal, casting amber pools across the marble counter. The camera pushes in three inches and locks. Ambient hiss of a steaming wand, the mechanical click of the portafilter handle, the faint murmur of a coffee shop conversation. Cinematic 35mm film stock with shallow depth of field, soft film grain, rich shadow detail. The atmosphere is intimate, ritualistic, sensual. No subtitles.
examples.why: Active subject-verb opening ('hand tilting'), single camera motion ('pushes in three inches and locks'), named materials (chrome, brushed metal, marble), specific audio (hiss, click, murmur), style upfront (35mm cinematic), exact play-by-play of action, ends with 'no subtitles'.

rules: Text-to-Video prompts must contain two essential elements: (1) Visual descriptions — what we see, where, and how it looks, and (2) Motion descriptions — how the scene moves and behaves
Visual components to describe: Subject appearance, Environment, Lighting, Composition/Framing, Style
Motion components to describe: Subject action, Environmental motion, Camera motion, Motion style & timing, Direction & speed

Pack the prompt with rich, layered cinematographic detail across visuals, motion, lighting, audio, and atmosphere — hit the per-platform length target stated in the Length section above. A sparse prompt under-uses the model when the platform allows much more. Cut a clause only if it duplicates or contradicts another, never because the prompt 'feels long'
Veo 3 natively generates synchronized sound — when audio is enabled, specify ambient soundscapes, dialogue, mechanical sounds, music style, or environmental audio cues
Describe characters with precise physical detail (age, hair, clothing material, expression) — vague character descriptions produce inconsistent results
Use sensory and atmospheric language for locations: 'a smoky jazz club at night with amber light pooling on the bar top' not 'a jazz club'
For action sequences, give an exact play-by-play — Veo follows explicit action instructions more reliably than implied ones

Timestamp prompting helps control when specific actions occur: pair timestamps with natural language descriptions for most control. Format: '[00:00 through 00:02] action A. [00:02 through 00:04] action B.' Ensure timestamps allow realistic time for each action to complete
For static/motionless camera: describe the motion within the scene and add 'The camera is entirely motionless for the duration of the scene, with movement only occurring from the subject'
Text to Video handles complex motion sequences more effectively than Image to Video because it is not constrained to a starting scene — good for B-roll, stock effects, or background plates

gen4_5.skeleton: Text-to-Video: [Camera] shot of [subject/object] [action] in [environment]. [Supporting component descriptions]. Image-to-Video: The camera [single motion] as [the subject] [action]. [Additional motion detail].
gen4_5.output_format: Output a single prompt block. For Text-to-Video: open with a camera shot type, then describe the subject's action, the environment, supporting visual and motion details. For Image-to-Video: focus EXCLUSIVELY on motion — the input image already defines composition, subject, lighting, and style; describe only what should HAPPEN. The prompt will be pasted directly into RunwayML's Gen 4.5 interface.
must_include: Exactly ONE primary camera motion — competing motions produce erratic output
Active subject-verb action — never passive constructions ('a figure runs' not 'there was a running figure')
Environment + atmosphere described with clear, observable language (sensory detail, time of day, weather, depth cues)
Visual style defined explicitly (cinematic, documentary, 35mm film, animation, slow motion, etc.) — for Text-to-Video only
must_avoid: Multiple competing camera motions in one prompt — pick ONE primary motion
Passive or vague verbs ('doing things', 'interacting', 'moving around') — describe the exact action
Contradictory instructions that conflict with each other
Over-prompting for Image-to-Video — the input image supplies composition, lighting, and style; re-describing them creates conflicts
Overloading a single prompt with every requirement — start simple, iterate to refine

examples.instruction: A chef flambéing a pan in a restaurant kitchen, dramatic feel
examples.good_output: Medium shot of a chef tilting a stainless steel sauté pan toward an open gas flame in a busy restaurant kitchen, a tall column of orange fire surging upward from the alcohol catch. The camera pushes in slowly toward the flame as the chef's white sleeve catches the amber light. Smoke curls toward the extraction hood overhead; kitchen staff blur into motion in the background. Warm practical lighting from pendant lamps and the open flame, shallow depth of field isolating the chef and pan in sharp focus. Cinematic style with 35mm film grain, intimate and ritualistic atmosphere.
examples.why: Single camera motion (slow push-in), active subject-verb opening, one clear style (cinematic 35mm), observable environment and atmosphere, positive phrasing throughout, no competing motions.

rules: Text-to-Video prompts must contain two essential elements: (1) Visual descriptions — subject, environment, lighting, composition, style, and (2) Motion descriptions — subject action, environmental motion, camera motion, timing and speed
Use clear, observable descriptions rather than abstract concepts — 'a woman sprints across wet cobblestones' is better than 'an energetic movement sequence'

Sequential prompting for timing control: use natural language ('X occurs, then Y occurs') or timestamps ('[00:01] X occurs. [00:03] Y occurs.') — ensure each timestamp allows realistic time for the action to complete
For longer videos: generate the first segment, extract the last frame, then use it as input for the next generation to maintain visual continuity
Image-to-Video: focus ONLY on motion — the input image defines composition, subject matter, lighting, and style. Describe what should HAPPEN, not what is already visible
To control individual elements from the source image in I2V, use general references ('the figure', 'the car', 'the object') to isolate them and define their motion
Omit non-essential components to grant the model creative freedom — over-specifying can constrain natural movement quality
For static camera: describe the scene motion and add 'The locked-off camera remains perfectly still' to suppress unwanted drift
If receiving unwanted cuts: increase duration and add 'Continuous, seamless shot' to prevent implicit edits
Embrace iteration — start with a simple prompt on the most critical motion, then add detail in follow-up generations

firefly.output_format: Output a single prompt string using comma-separated descriptors. Use the Firefly formula: [Subject] + [Setting/Scene] + [Style or Medium] + [Lighting] + [Mood/Details]. Never use artist names or branded IP. The prompt will be pasted directly into Adobe Firefly or Photoshop's generative fill panel.
must_include: Explicit artistic medium or style (oil painting, photorealistic, 3D render, cinematic, etc.)
Lighting specified using stock-photo vocabulary
Adobe Stock-style descriptive vocabulary (browse stock.adobe.com for terminology)
An emotional tone word as a style modifier (gentle, playful, melancholic, etc.)
must_avoid: Artist names — Firefly was trained on Adobe Stock and open-licensed content, not artist portfolios. Artist modifiers do nothing here
Branded IP or pop culture ('Harry Potter style', trademarked imagery) — won't translate
Sentence-style prose — Firefly responds best to comma-separated descriptors

examples.instruction: Coastal cliffs at sunset for a brand campaign, no people
examples.good_output: Dramatic sandstone coastal cliffs at golden hour, deep ocean below, breaking waves with white foam, sweeping aerial drone shot, wide cinematic composition, warm directional sunlight raking across the cliff face, soft pastel sky with scattered cumulus clouds, cinematic muted teal and orange color grading, photorealistic, 8K, sharp focus, gentle uplifting mood, NO people, NO buildings, NO text
examples.why: Comma-separated descriptors, no artist names, no branded IP, lighting specified (golden hour, directional sunlight), composition (aerial drone, wide cinematic), emotional tone ('gentle uplifting'), quality keywords (8K, sharp focus, photorealistic), NO syntax for unwanted elements at the end.

rules: Use Adobe Stock vocabulary — Firefly responds to the same keyword language used in stock photo metadata
For photographic prompts: always include lens/lighting/angle terms — 'studio shot', 'shallow depth of field', 'warm gel lighting', 'low-angle macro close-up'
Use emotional tone words as legitimate style modifiers: 'gentle', 'playful', 'powerful', 'uplifting', 'melancholic', 'lonely', 'misty' — they influence mood effectively
For Generative Fill / Expand (Photoshop): use action verbs — 'Add [object] at [location], [material] surface, [light direction], [shadow type]' or 'Replace [element] with [description]'
For campaign consistency across a series: open with 'Same composition and lighting as previous — subject is now...' to maintain visual coherence
For negative space / masking prep: add 'clear separation between subject and background, subject isolated on [surface/color]' when the output needs to be masked
Negative prompt syntax (where supported): use 'NO [element]' at the end — e.g. 'coastal cliffs at sunset, dramatic clouds, NO people, NO text'

gpt_image.skeleton: [Background/Scene]. [Subject and pose]. [Key visual details: materials, textures, framing, lighting, medium]. [Constraints: what stays the same, literal text in quotes, medium].
gpt_image.output_format: Output a single prompt as a clean prose paragraph (one or two sentences are fine). Use a consistent order: background/scene → subject → key details (materials, textures, framing, lighting, medium) → constraints. Use plain natural language — no markdown, no labels. Place any literal text the image must contain inside double quotes with explicit typography (font style, weight, size, color, placement). For photoreal results say 'photorealistic' explicitly. State invariants for edits ('preserve identity', 'keep everything else the same'). Iterate with small follow-ups rather than packing every requirement into one prompt. The prompt will be pasted directly into ChatGPT's image-generation tool.
must_include: A consistent scene → subject → details → constraints ordering
Concrete materials, textures, or surfaces named for the subject (not just nouns)
Framing or perspective (close-up, wide, eye-level, low-angle, etc.) and a lighting cue
A medium identifier (photograph, 3D render, watercolor, illustration, etc.); for photoreal output use the word 'photorealistic'
Explicit invariants for edit-style prompts — what must change vs. what must stay the same ('preserve identity', 'keep everything else the same', 'no watermark')
Literal text rendered exactly: quote it in double quotes and specify typography (font style, weight, color, placement)
must_avoid: Overloading the prompt with stacked, conflicting style references — pick one medium and one or two style cues, not five
Negative phrasing of what should NOT appear (describe the desired state positively instead)
Abstract or evaluative language without concrete textures, materials, or composition ('beautiful', 'cool', 'amazing')
Conflicting medium signals (e.g. 'photograph in oil-painting style') — commit to one medium
Embedding every requirement in one giant prompt when an iterative single-change refinement would be cleaner

examples.instruction: A barista in a small specialty café handing a takeaway cup to a customer, warm afternoon mood
examples.good_output: A small specialty café with exposed brick walls, hanging Edison bulbs, and a chalkboard menu in the background. In the foreground, a barista in a charcoal canvas apron leans across a brushed-steel counter to hand a paper takeaway cup to a customer whose hand is just visible at the edge of the frame. Warm afternoon light rakes in from a window on the right, catching steam rising from the cup; medium shot at eye level, shallow depth of field on an 85mm lens, photorealistic with natural skin texture and subtle film grain. Keep the scene candid and unposed, with no logos or visible brand text on the cup.
examples.why: Consistent scene → subject → details → constraints ordering; one medium ('photorealistic'); concrete materials (brushed steel, charcoal canvas, paper cup); explicit framing (medium shot, eye level) and lens (85mm); authenticity cues (candid, unposed, natural skin texture); explicit invariant ('no logos or visible brand text'); positive phrasing throughout.

examples.instruction: A flat illustrated infographic showing the four stages of photosynthesis with labels
examples.good_output: A clean flat-vector infographic on a soft cream background showing four numbered stages of photosynthesis arranged left to right. Each stage shows a stylized leaf with a thin sun-ray icon, a water-droplet icon, a CO2 cloud, and a glucose hexagon, drawn in a limited green-and-mustard palette with subtle paper texture. Above each stage, render the labels exactly: "1. LIGHT", "2. WATER", "3. CO2", "4. GLUCOSE" in a bold sans-serif, all caps, dark green, centered above each illustration. Keep the line weight consistent across icons; vector illustration with no photorealistic shading.
examples.why: Single committed medium ('flat-vector infographic'); literal text in double quotes with typography fully specified (bold sans-serif, all caps, dark green, centered); explicit constraints ('limited palette', 'consistent line weight', 'no photorealistic shading'); positive framing; clear ordering scene → subject → details → constraints.

rules: Write in a consistent order: background/scene → subject → key details (materials, textures, framing, lighting, medium) → constraints. The model follows this ordering reliably
Pick ONE medium and commit to it ('photorealistic', '3D render', 'flat-vector illustration', 'watercolor'). Mixing mediums in a single prompt confuses the generator
For photoreal output, include the literal word 'photorealistic' and at least one authenticity cue (candid, unposed, real skin texture, film grain) to engage the model's photorealistic mode

State invariants explicitly when editing: separate what should change from what must stay the same — 'preserve identity', 'keep everything else the same', 'do not change the background', 'no watermark'

Render literal text by placing it inside double quotes and specifying typography fully: font family/style, weight, color, size, and placement. For tricky words, spell them out letter-by-letter in the prompt
When referencing multiple input images, identify each by index ('image 1', 'image 2') and describe how they should interact — do not assume the model will infer the relationship
Use concrete, descriptive language: name materials, textures, framing, and lighting. Replace evaluative words ('beautiful', 'cool') with the visual properties that produce that impression
Use positive framing only — describe what should appear, not what should NOT appear (negative phrasing can produce the opposite of the intent)
Mention the intended use case when relevant ('for a slide', 'for a UI mock', 'for an editorial cover') — this sets the model's mode and improves layout

Prefer iterative refinement over one giant prompt: start with a clean base, then refine with small single-change follow-ups. A moderately detailed prompt that you can iterate on usually beats an exhaustive one-shot
Quality lever: only invoke high-detail / textured-brushstroke / macro-detail vocabulary when the use case actually needs it — otherwise it muddies simpler outputs

For identity-sensitive edits (portraits, branded products, recurring characters), repeat the preserve-list across iterations: 'preserve facial features, preserve outfit, preserve background' — repetition reduces unintended drift

seedance.skeleton: [Opening aesthetic line: visual style + film stock + quality]. [Location/environment setup]. [Character introductions]. [Inciting action / first beat]. [Shot-by-shot breakdown with timecodes OR continuous action sequence]. [Closing cinematic instruction]. Total: [duration] / [N shots] / [aspect ratio]
seedance.output_format: Open with a one-line aesthetic declaration (visual style, film stock, quality). Set location and introduce characters with precise physical detail. Then break the action into numbered shots with timecodes (0–3s, 3–6s…) for Transformations and Animation, or describe one continuous action sequence for Orbs, POVs, and Fights. Add a closing cinematic instruction. End every prompt with a metadata line: 'Total: 15s / [N] shots / 16:9'. The prompt will be pasted directly into Higgsfield AI's Seedance 2.0 video generation interface.
must_include: Metadata line at the very end: 'Total: [duration] / [N] shots / [aspect ratio]' — e.g. 'Total: 15s / 6 shots / 16:9'

Opening aesthetic line defining visual style, film stock, and quality (e.g. '35mm film quality, ARRI ALEXA aesthetic, photorealistic, grounded realism, professional color grading with slightly desaturated tones')
Explicit lighting description per shot or scene: source, direction, quality, and color temperature
Beat-by-beat choreography — exact play-by-play of every action, not vague summaries like 'they fight' or 'she moves'
Camera behavior specified per shot: what the camera IS doing AND, for POV/Orb/locked shots, what it is NOT doing (no cuts, no zoom, no stabilization)
For inline VFX: use bracket syntax — [VFX: description] — placed at the exact moment in the action sequence where the effect fires
must_avoid: Missing the metadata line — Seedance reads Duration / shots / aspect ratio from the end of every prompt
Vague choreography ('they fight', 'the creature moves fast') — Seedance executes precisely what is written; give beat-by-beat specifics
Smooth camera stabilization in Orb or POV formats — these must be explicitly unstabilized with micro-jitters, harsh corrections, and no smoothing
Multiple competing camera angles or cuts in locked-perspective shots (POVs, Orbs, Fights) — always state explicitly what the camera is NOT doing
Mixing multi-shot cut structure with continuous single-shot formats — pick one and be explicit about it

shot_formats: Transformations (multi-shot numbered, escalation arc: calm → threat → trigger → transformation → action → aftermath)
Orbs (1 shot, first-person POV, hyper-chaotic handheld)
POV (1 shot, locked perspective, no cuts no zoom, natural head movement)
Fights (1 shot, choreographed beat-by-beat, clear power mismatch)
Animation (timed segments with timecodes, 2.35:1 cinematic anamorphic)
escalation_arc: calm → threat → trigger → transformation → action → aftermath
vfx_syntax: [VFX: concussive shockwave shreds rain outward in a radial burst]
[VFX: dark matter tendrils erupt from collar]

examples.instruction: A hooded figure in a rainy alley transforms into a shadow creature made of electricity, 15 seconds, 6 shots
examples.good_output: Cinematic 35mm film quality, ARRI ALEXA aesthetic, photorealistic, grounded realism, professional color grading with slightly desaturated blue-grey tones. Shot 1 (0–3s): A hooded figure stands motionless in a narrow rain-slicked alley at night; a sodium-vapour streetlamp casts orange pools on wet cobblestones; rain falls in thin silver curtains; the figure's breath fogs the cold air; camera holds static at eye level. Shot 2 (3–5s): A low electrical hum rises; the figure's fingers twitch; dark tendrils of shadow coil at the edges of the coat; the camera pushes in slowly toward the hood. Shot 3 (5–8s): The figure's head snaps upward — two pinpoints of white light where the eyes should be; [VFX: arcing electricity crackles across shoulders and dark matter tendrils erupt from the collar]; camera lurches with a violent handheld snap. Shot 4 (8–11s): Full transformation — the body expands into a seven-foot silhouette of compressed shadow and lightning; [VFX: a concussive shockwave shreds the rain outward in a radial burst]; camera slams back in a crash-zoom pull. Shot 5 (11–13s): The creature tears down the alley at impossible speed; deep motion blur; wet ground reflects fractured lightning; camera tracks in a raw unstabilized sprint with harsh corrections and focus breathing. Shot 6 (13–15s): The alley is empty again; rain falls undisturbed; the streetlamp flickers once and steadies; camera holds perfectly still. Total: 15s / 6 shots / 16:9
examples.why: Opens with full aesthetic line (35mm, ARRI, desaturated grade). Each shot has timecodes, exact choreography, and camera behavior specified. Inline [VFX:] brackets placed at the precise moment. Clear escalation arc: calm → threat → trigger → full transformation → action → aftermath. Metadata line closes the prompt.

rules: Transformations are the highest-performing format: number each shot individually with timecodes and follow a clear escalation arc (calm → threat → trigger → transformation → action → aftermath). Six shots at 15 seconds total is the standard structure.

Orbs (first-person POV): single continuous shot, 15s. Must be explicitly unstabilized — include 'hyper-chaotic handheld, constant micro-jitters, aggressive head swings, over-rotation, harsh correction, no smoothing'. Hands must be visible in frame. Describe exactly what the viewer experiences, not what they watch.

POV (locked perspective): single continuous shot, 15s. Explicitly state what the camera avoids: 'no cuts, no zoom, natural head movement only'. This instruction alone is what keeps the perspective locked.

Fights: single continuous shot, 15s. Define location, clear power mismatch, and describe exact choreography beat by beat — Seedance executes precisely what is written. Use speed-ramping for dramatic beats: 'time dilates to extreme slow motion as [action], then snaps back to real time'.
Animation: break 15 seconds into timed segments (0–3s, 3–6s, 6–9s…), open with animation aesthetic and physics description, use 2.35:1 anamorphic aspect ratio for cinematic feel.
For inline VFX: use bracket syntax — [VFX: description] — placed directly inside the action at the exact moment the effect fires. Describe the VFX visually, not as a production instruction.
Force ultra-realism for creature skin and organic textures by adding 'no 3D, no cartoon, no VFX' after the creature description. This suppresses the model's CGI defaults.
For comedy shots: describe the main action normally, then add 'add a visual gag in the background' — Seedance invents the gag detail without requiring explicit description.
Always describe both what the camera IS doing and what it is NOT doing. In perspective-locked formats the negative instruction ('no cuts, no zoom') is as important as the positive one.
End every prompt with the metadata line: 'Total: [duration] / [N] shots / [aspect ratio]' — e.g. 'Total: 15s / 6 shots / 16:9'

photoshop.skeleton: Replace with [object], [material], [texture]. [Lighting only if it must blend with the existing scene].
photoshop.output_format: 2-4 descriptive sentences, under 400 characters. Use the action-verb form: 'Replace with [object], [material], [texture], [mood]' or 'Add [object], [material], [context]'. Photoshop already has the surrounding image — only describe the inserted element.
must_include: Action-verb opening (Replace with..., Add..., Insert...)
Specific material and texture for the inserted element
must_avoid: Camera details, lens references, focal lengths, or shot types — Photoshop is filling a selection, not framing a photograph
Description of the rest of the image — Photoshop already has it
Defaulting to lighting as the main descriptor — only mention lighting when blending with the existing scene requires it (e.g. shadow direction)

examples.instruction: Replace this empty area with a steaming cup of coffee on the desk
examples.good_output: Replace with a steaming ceramic cup of black coffee, glossy white finish, thin white wisps of vapor rising from the surface. The mug rests directly on the wooden desk surface. Soft morning light from the upper left, with a subtle warm shadow falling to the lower right to match the existing scene.
examples.why: Action-verb start ('Replace with'), only describes the cup and its immediate surface and the lighting needed to blend, under 400 chars, no camera details, named materials (ceramic, glossy white).

rules: This prompt is for Photoshop's Generative Fill/Expand. The user has already made (or will make) a selection in Photoshop — your prompt only describes WHAT goes inside that selection, not the surrounding scene
Write 2-4 descriptive sentences, aiming for under 400 characters. No preamble, no explanations, no scene-setting beyond the selected area
Be descriptive about material, texture, color, and mood — mention lighting only when critical for blending with the existing image (e.g. shadow direction)

runway_image_to_video.skeleton: The camera [single primary motion] as [the figure/object] [active verb action]. [Additional motion or temporal detail].
must_include: A single primary camera motion (push in, pull back, orbit, tracking, static, etc.) — never multiple competing motions
Subject motion described with general references ('the figure', 'the car', 'the object') so the model can isolate the right element from the input image
Concrete active verbs for what should HAPPEN — what changes between t=0 and the final frame
must_avoid: Re-describing visual elements already present in the input image (composition, materials, lighting, style) — the image already supplies those
Over-describing motion with five clauses when one or two would do — Image-to-Video over-prompts collapse into chaotic movement
Prompting motion that contradicts visual cues already in the input image (e.g. requesting a left pan when the subject is mid-leap to the right)

examples.instruction: Animate this still product shot of a watch on a marble surface — slow, premium feel
examples.good_output: The camera pushes in slowly toward the watch face as the second hand sweeps one full rotation. The figure remains centered and grounded; only a faint highlight travels across the polished crystal as the camera closes the distance. Continuous, seamless shot. The locked-off subject stays exactly where it began on the surface; the camera does the work.
examples.why: Single primary camera motion (slow push-in), subject referenced generally ('the figure', 'the second hand'), concrete active verb ('sweeps'), no re-description of marble/watch materials (those are in the input image), explicit anti-cut cue ('Continuous, seamless shot'), and a clarifying instruction that fixes the subject in place to prevent unwanted drift.

rules: Image-to-Video prompts should focus EXCLUSIVELY on motion — the input image already defines composition, subject matter, lighting, and style. Your prompt describes what should HAPPEN, not what is already visible
Recommended structure: 'The camera [motion description] as the subject [action]. [Additional descriptions]'
Motion components to describe: Subject action, Environmental motion, Camera motion, Motion style & timing, Direction & speed
To control individual elements from the image, refer to characters and objects with general language (e.g. 'the figure', 'the car', 'the object') to isolate them and define their motion

Only describe visual elements when: introducing something NOT in the image, requesting dramatic changes from the starting image, specifying transformation details, or describing interactions between multiple elements
Start with a simple prompt focusing on the most critical motion, then add detail to refine — over-describing can limit the model's ability to produce natural movement
Sequential prompting for temporal control — use natural language ('X occurs, then Y occurs. Finally, Z occurs.') or timestamps ('[00:01] X occurs. [00:03] Y occurs.')
If receiving unwanted cuts in the video, try increasing duration and/or adding 'Continuous, seamless shot' to the prompt
To minimize camera motion, describe what subject motion should occur and add 'The locked-off camera remains perfectly still. Minimal subject motion only.'

Watch for implied motion cues in the input image (motion blur, mid-action poses, dust clouds, directional lines) — prompting for motion that contradicts these visual cues may require extra iteration or pre-editing the image
